content:
2026-10-12

- [ ] Prep notes for [[Work/Billing refactor notes|billing refactor]] 1:1 with Marcus on Wed
- [ ] Finish [[System Design/Rate limiter|rate limiter]] re-read before sprint planning
- On-call rotation starts next Mon (Oct 19) — reread [[Work/On-call runbook notes]].
- Cooked stir-fry tonight, finally nailed the velveting step.

2026-10-09

- Friday wrap at 5:30, dinner with Jake at Pike Street Grill.
- Shipped the pagination fix on billing-api. Notes in [[Work/Billing refactor notes]].
- Idea: apply [[Go/Worker pools]] pattern to Ridgeline's link-fetcher backlog.

Weekly reflection — 2026-W41

## What went well
- Got the billing pagination bug fixed and reviewed. See [[Work/Billing refactor notes]].
- Re-read [[System Design/Rate limiter]] and actually understood the sliding-window-counter tradeoff this time.

## What was hard
- Context cancellation propagation bit me again in a goroutine leak. Reviewed [[Go/Context package]].

## Next week
- On-call rotation Oct 19-25. Skim [[Work/On-call runbook notes]] Friday.
- Keep chipping at [[Ridgeline/Architecture decisions]] — the queue choice is still open.

## Personal
- Feeling more settled at Ridgeline. A year in and I can actually navigate the codebase without grep panic.

Weekly reflection — 2026-W36

## What went well
- Finished [[Databases/Postgres indexing]] notes and applied a partial index that dropped a slow query from 1.2s to 40ms.
- Paired with Priya on the K8s operator; [[Infra/K8s operators]] notes helped.

## What was hard
- Still shaky on transaction isolation. Went back to [[DDIA/Ch7 Transactions]].

## Next week
- Start [[System Design/Rate limiter]] design study.
- Ridgeline: decide on the search backend.

Go concurrency patterns

Notes from daily work + The Go Programming Language. Related: [[Go/Worker pools]], [[Go/Context package]].

## Fan-out / fan-in
- Spawn N workers reading from one channel, merge results into one output channel.
- Always close the input channel from the producer side, never the consumer.

## Pipeline
- Each stage is a function taking an inbound channel and returning an outbound one.
- Use a done channel (or context.Context) to signal cancellation downstream.

## sync primitives
- sync.WaitGroup for waiting on a known set of goroutines.
- sync.Once for lazy init. errgroup.Group when any goroutine error should cancel the rest.

## Gotchas
- Loop variable capture in goroutines (pre-1.22) — pass as arg.
- Leaked goroutines blocked on a channel nobody writes to. See [[Go/Context package]].

Go error handling

## Wrapping
- fmt.Errorf("doing X: %w", err) to wrap; errors.Is / errors.As to inspect.
- Wrap with context at each layer, but don't wrap then immediately log — pick one.

## Sentinel errors
- Define var ErrNotFound = errors.New("not found") for expected branches.
- Prefer typed errors when callers need fields (e.g. a ValidationError with a field name).

## At the boundary
- HTTP handlers translate domain errors to status codes in one place.
- Used this in Ridgeline — see [[Ridgeline/Architecture decisions]].

## Don't
- Don't panic for ordinary control flow. Reserve panic for truly unrecoverable init.

Go context package

## Rules of thumb
- Pass ctx as the first arg, never store it in a struct.
- context.Background() at the top (main, tests); context.TODO() when unsure.
- Always defer cancel() after WithCancel / WithTimeout to avoid leaks.

## Cancellation
- Select on ctx.Done() in any blocking loop. Return ctx.Err().
- This is how you stop the goroutine leaks noted in [[Go/Concurrency patterns]].

## Values
- context.WithValue only for request-scoped data (trace ids), not optional params.

## Real example
- Ridgeline's link-fetcher uses a 10s WithTimeout per outbound fetch.

Go worker pools

A bounded pool to cap concurrency. Builds on [[Go/Concurrency patterns]].

go
jobs := make(chan Job)
for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
    go worker(ctx, jobs, results)
}


## Notes
- Size the pool to the bottleneck (CPU vs IO). For Ridgeline's fetcher (IO-bound) I run ~20 workers.
- Use context for shutdown — see [[Go/Context package]].
- Backpressure comes free from the unbuffered jobs channel.

Postgres indexing

## B-tree (default)
- Good for equality + range. Leftmost-prefix rule for composite indexes.

## Partial indexes
- CREATE INDEX ... WHERE status = 'active' — huge win for the billing table at work.
- Dropped a hot query from 1.2s to 40ms (see [[Reflections/Weekly 2026-W36]]).

## Covering / INCLUDE
- Index-only scans when all selected cols are in the index.

## GIN
- For jsonb and full-text. Ridgeline tag search uses a GIN index on tags.

## Check with
- EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). More in [[Databases/Postgres tuning notes]].

Postgres tuning notes

- work_mem is per-operation × connections — beware OOM on bursty workloads.
- shared_buffers: 25% of RAM as a starting point.
- effective_cache_size: ~50-75% of RAM, hint for the planner.
- Vacuum/analyze after bulk loads; watch for bloat.
- Connection pooling via pgbouncer for the homelab Ridgeline DB.
- Indexing strategy lives in [[Databases/Postgres indexing]].

Kubernetes operators

## Pattern
- CRD defines desired state; controller reconciles actual -> desired in a loop.
- Reconcile must be idempotent and level-triggered (not edge-triggered).

## Building blocks
- controller-runtime (Go). Watch -> queue -> Reconcile(req).
- Set owner references so child objects get garbage collected.

## Homelab
- Ran one on my homelab k8s cluster to manage Ridgeline deploys. See [[Ridgeline/Architecture decisions]].

## Gotchas
- Don't block in Reconcile; requeue with backoff instead.

Terraform modules

## Structure
- A module = inputs (variables), resources, outputs. Keep them small and composable.
- Pin provider + module versions. Remote state in S3 with DynamoDB lock at work.

## Patterns
- Use for_each over count when items have stable keys (avoids destroy/recreate churn).
- terraform plan in CI, apply gated on review.

## Homelab
- I keep a small module for the homelab DNS + Cloudflare records.

AWS Lambda notes

## Cold starts
- Go binaries cold-start fast (~50-100ms). Provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive paths.

## Patterns
- Keep handlers thin; init clients outside the handler for reuse across invocations.
- Idempotency keys for anything triggered by SQS/SNS (at-least-once delivery).

## At work
- A few Lambdas handle webhook ingestion feeding the billing pipeline. See [[Work/Billing refactor notes]].

Rate limiter design

## Algorithms
- Token bucket: smooth, allows bursts up to bucket size.
- Leaky bucket: smooths output, no bursts.
- Fixed window: simple but boundary spikes.
- Sliding window counter: best balance — finally clicked this week (see [[Reflections/Weekly 2026-W41]]).

## Distributed
- Redis with Lua for atomic check-and-decrement.
- Consistent hashing to shard limiter keys — see [[System Design/Consistent hashing]].

## Ridgeline
- Per-API-key token bucket on the public Ridgeline API.

URL shortener design

## Core
- Generate short code: base62 of an auto-increment id, or a hash + collision check.
- KV store maps code -> long URL. Read-heavy, cache aggressively.

## Scale
- Partition by code prefix; see [[System Design/Consistent hashing]].
- 301 vs 302: use 302 if you want click analytics.

## Notes
- Ridgeline reuses a similar short-code scheme for shareable bookmark links.

Consistent hashing

## Why
- Adding/removing a node only remaps ~1/N of keys instead of all.

## How
- Hash nodes + keys onto a ring; key goes to the next node clockwise.
- Virtual nodes to even out the distribution.

## Used in
- Distributed [[System Design/Rate limiter]] sharding.
- DDIA covers this under partitioning — see [[DDIA/Ch6 Partitioning]].

DDIA Ch5 — Replication

## Leader-based
- Single leader, async vs sync replication. Sync = durable but slow; async = fast but can lose writes on failover.

## Replication lag problems
- Read-your-writes, monotonic reads, consistent prefix reads.

## Multi-leader / leaderless
- Conflict resolution (LWW, version vectors). Quorum reads/writes (w + r > n).

## Connects to
- Partitioning in [[DDIA/Ch6 Partitioning]] and transactions in [[DDIA/Ch7 Transactions]].

DDIA Ch6 — Partitioning

## Strategies
- By key range: efficient range scans, risk of hot spots.
- By hash of key: even distribution, loses range queries.

## Rebalancing
- Fixed number of partitions; dynamic partitioning. Avoid hash-mod-N (remaps everything).
- This is exactly the [[System Design/Consistent hashing]] motivation.

## Secondary indexes
- Local (document-partitioned) vs global (term-partitioned).

## See also
- [[DDIA/Ch5 Replication]].

DDIA Ch7 — Transactions

## ACID
- The 'I' is the slippery one. Isolation levels trade safety for performance.

## Isolation levels
- Read committed -> snapshot isolation (MVCC) -> serializable.
- Anomalies: dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantoms, write skew.

## Serializable
- Actual serial execution, 2PL, or SSI (serializable snapshot isolation).

## Notes
- Postgres uses MVCC for snapshot isolation. Tuning in [[Databases/Postgres tuning notes]].
- Came back to this twice — see [[Reflections/Weekly 2026-W36]].

Ridgeline architecture decisions

Self-hosted read-it-later tool. Go + Postgres backend, React/Tailwind frontend, homelab k8s.

## ADR-001: Monolith first
- Single Go binary. Split later only if needed. No premature microservices.

## ADR-002: Postgres for everything
- Bookmarks, tags (GIN index), full-text search. Schema in [[Ridgeline/Postgres schema notes]].

## ADR-003: Link fetcher
- Bounded [[Go/Worker pools]] with per-fetch [[Go/Context package]] timeouts.

## Open
- [ ] Job queue: in-Postgres (SKIP LOCKED) vs Redis. Leaning Postgres to avoid another moving part.
- [ ] Search: keep Postgres FTS or add a dedicated engine.

Ridgeline Postgres schema notes

## Tables
- bookmarks(id, user_id, url, title, created_at, archived)
- tags(id, name), bookmark_tags(bookmark_id, tag_id)

## Indexes
- GIN on a tsvector of title+description for FTS.
- Partial index WHERE archived = false for the active list. Pattern from [[Databases/Postgres indexing]].

## Notes
- SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED candidate for the fetch queue (see [[Ridgeline/Architecture decisions]]).

On-call runbook notes

My personal cheatsheet before the Oct 19-25 rotation.

## First response
- Ack the PagerDuty page, check Datadog dashboard, look at the recent deploys.
- Communicate early in the incident channel, even just 'investigating'.

## Common pages
- billing-api p95 latency -> check DB connections + slow queries ([[Databases/Postgres indexing]]).
- Webhook backlog -> Lambda concurrency limit ([[Infra/AWS Lambda notes]]).

## Escalation
- Dave owns billing-service; Marcus is the manager escalation.
- Postmortem template in the team wiki.

Billing refactor notes

Context for the Oct 14 1:1 with Marcus.

## Done
- Fixed the pagination bug (off-by-one on the cursor) — shipped Oct 9.

## In progress
- Extracting the invoice calculation into a pure function so it's unit-testable.
- Error wrapping cleanup using patterns from [[Go/Error handling]].

## Risks
- The webhook ingestion Lambdas need idempotency keys ([[Infra/AWS Lambda notes]]).

The Go Programming Language — notes

Working through Donovan & Kernighan in my first months at Ridgeline.

## Ch8-9: Goroutines & channels
- This is the foundation for [[Go/Concurrency patterns]].
- select, buffered vs unbuffered, the happens-before guarantees.

## Ch5: Functions
- deferred calls, error handling idioms -> [[Go/Error handling]].

## Takeaway
- The language is small on purpose. Re-reading beats skimming.

First month at Ridgeline

Started Aug 2025 as a junior backend eng. Writing this to remember the beginner feeling.

## Wins
- Shipped my first PR (a small log-level fix) in week two.
- Marcus is a patient manager; Priya has been a great unofficial mentor.

## Hard
- The Go codebase is bigger than anything from school. Reading [[Books/Go Programming Language notes]] on the side.
- Imposter syndrome is real, but everyone googles things.

## Goal
- By next summer, own a service end to end. (Update: a year in and billing is basically mine.)

- idea: add SKIP LOCKED job queue to Ridgeline instead of Redis ([[Ridgeline/Architecture decisions]])
- TODO: ask Dave about billing-service escalation path before on-call week
- read: that blog post on Postgres index-only scans
- gift idea: mechanical keyboard kit for Jake's birthday (Oct 17)
